summary:
Hydrofluoric acid causes liquefactive necrosis instead of coagulative necrosis when it comes into contact with tissue; thus, it meets the criteria specified for answering which acid avoids showing typical coagulative necrotizing effects observed from other stronger acids such as HCl and \(H_2SO_4\). The focus remains solely on distinguishing between these two types of cellular damage mechanisms induced by different chemicals.
``` Summary:

Hydrofluoric acid induces liquefactive necrosis without causing the characteristic coagulative necrosis associated with other strong acids like hydrochloric and sulfuric acids. This distinction makes hydrogen fluoride unique regarding its effect on tissues compared to those caused by more common industrial acids.
```
